does: This paper introduces THREAT, a framework that automatically discovers jailbreak prompts for large language models through coordinated multi-model reasoning and iterative optimization. The method formulates prompt discovery as a nonconvex optimization problem and employs an efficient search algorithm that iteratively refines adversarial prompts across multiple LLMs working together. THREAT achieves substantially higher attack success rates than prior methods—reducing detection by safety filters to under 1% compared to roughly 50% refusals for unmodified prompts—while simultaneously reducing computational requirements. The framework systematically explores the vulnerability landscape of aligned LLMs across diverse datasets and model architectures, demonstrating that state-of-the-art safety mechanisms remain brittle against coordinated adversarial reframing attacks.
connects: This work extends a growing body of research on adversarial robustness and jailbreaking of aligned LLMs, building on prior prompt-based attack methods that exploit model vulnerabilities through creative instruction manipulation. The paper responds to limitations in earlier single-model or gradient-based attack strategies by introducing a multi-model coordination approach inspired by ensemble reasoning and collaborative problem-solving. It connects to broader research on adversarial optimization in machine learning, adapting nonconvex optimization techniques to the discrete, language-based setting of prompt discovery. The research also engages with the literature on safety alignment failures, showing how iterative refinement and reasoning-driven tactics can circumvent current defense mechanisms that were designed against simpler attacks.
enables: This work establishes THREAT as a diagnostic and red-teaming tool for strengthening LLM safety through proactive vulnerability discovery. By revealing previously undetected failure modes in aligned models, it enables researchers to design more robust safety mechanisms that account for sophisticated, reasoning-driven adversarial strategies rather than surface-level perturbations. The framework's efficiency and generality across model architectures position it for systematic safety evaluation in the development of future foundation models. The findings suggest follow-up directions in adversarial training, defensive prompt engineering, and architectural changes to reduce susceptibility to reframing-based attacks. More broadly, the work motivates deeper investigation into the fundamental alignment problem and the gap between stated safety objectives and actual model behavior under coordinated adversarial pressure.
